atomic-structure/atoms-elements-compound.summary: Define atoms, elements and compounds and explain the differences between them.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between a compound and a mixture?
opts: In a compound, elements are chemically bonded in fixed proportions — a mixture is not bonded and can be separated physically.
A compound contains only one type of atom. A mixture contains two or more types.
A mixture is always a liquid. A compound is always a solid.
They are the same — both describe combinations of different substances.

wrong_explanations.1: One type of atom = ELEMENT. Compounds contain two or more elements chemically bonded.
wrong_explanations.2: Mixtures and compounds can be solids, liquids or gases — state does not define them.
wrong_explanations.3: Compounds and mixtures are fundamentally different — chemical bonding is the key distinction.

quiz.q: A student heats a mixture of iron filings and sulfur. A grey solid forms that cannot be separated using a magnet. What has formed?
opts: A compound — iron sulfide — formed by chemical bonding between iron and sulfur
A mixture — the iron and sulfur have blended more thoroughly
An element — the sulfur has been destroyed leaving only iron
A solution — the sulfur has dissolved into the iron

wrong_explanations.1: If it were still a mixture, the iron could be separated magnetically. Since it cannot, a chemical reaction has occurred.
wrong_explanations.2: Elements cannot be destroyed by chemical reactions — only rearranged into compounds.
wrong_explanations.3: Sulfur is a solid at these temperatures — it does not dissolve into iron.

quiz.q: Which equation is correctly balanced?
opts: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O
H₂ + O₂ → H₂O
H₂ + O₂ → H₂O₂
2H₂ + 2O₂ → 2H₂O

wrong_explanations.1: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O: left has 2 O atoms, right has only 1 O atom — not balanced.
wrong_explanations.2: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O₂: this equation is balanced (2H and 2O each side) but produces hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), not water. The question asks about water formation.
wrong_explanations.3: 2H₂ + 2O₂ → 2H₂O: left has 4 O atoms, right has only 2 O atoms — not balanced.

quiz.q: Which of these is a mixture?
opts: Bronze — copper and tin metals mixed together, not chemically bonded
Water — hydrogen and oxygen in a fixed 2:1 ratio
Sodium chloride — sodium and chlorine in ionic bonds
Carbon dioxide — carbon and oxygen in covalent bonds

wrong_explanations.1: Water is a COMPOUND — hydrogen and oxygen are chemically bonded. Electrolysis is needed to separate them.
wrong_explanations.2: Sodium chloride is a COMPOUND — ionic bonds between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions.
wrong_explanations.3: Carbon dioxide is a COMPOUND — covalent bonds between C and O atoms.

foundation.common_mistake: A COMPOUND is NOT a mixture. In a compound, elements are CHEMICALLY BONDED — you need a chemical reaction to separate them (e.g. electrolysis to split water into hydrogen and oxygen). In a MIXTURE, substances are just mixed — physical methods separate them. Water is a compound. Saltwater is a mixture.

atomic-structure/mixtures.summary: Describe physical methods for separating mixtures and the principles behind each.

quiz.q: A student wants to obtain pure water from seawater. Which technique should they use?
opts: Simple distillation — heat the seawater and condense the steam
Filtration — filter through paper to remove the salt
Chromatography — water travels faster than dissolved salts
Crystallisation — cool the seawater to crystallise the water

wrong_explanations.1: Filtration only removes INSOLUBLE solids — salt is dissolved in seawater so passes straight through filter paper.
wrong_explanations.2: Chromatography separates dissolved substances by their travel speed — it does not separate water from dissolved salts.
wrong_explanations.3: Crystallisation removes water to leave the SALT — the opposite of what is needed here.

quiz.q: In a chromatography experiment, a spot travels 4.5 cm and the solvent front travels 9.0 cm. What is the Rf value?
opts: 13.5 — adding the two distances
4.5 — using just the distance the spot moved

wrong_explanations.1: Rf = 9.0 ÷ 4.5 = 2.0 — this is dividing the WRONG WAY. Rf is always substance ÷ solvent, and must be between 0 and 1.
wrong_explanations.2: Adding distances has no meaning. Rf = substance distance ÷ solvent distance = 4.5 ÷ 9.0 = 0.50.
wrong_explanations.3: Just the distance alone gives no useful information — Rf is a ratio, which is why it has no units.

quiz.q: Why is fractional distillation used to separate crude oil rather than simple distillation?
opts: Crude oil contains many liquids with different boiling points — fractional distillation separates them into different fractions
Crude oil is a solid — simple distillation cannot be used on solids
The fractions in crude oil are insoluble in each other — filtration would work just as well
Simple distillation uses too much energy for industrial use

wrong_explanations.1: Crude oil is a LIQUID mixture — simple distillation collects one fraction at a time but cannot separate all components simultaneously.
wrong_explanations.2: The fractions IN crude oil mix readily — filtration only works for insoluble particles, not dissolved or miscible liquids.
wrong_explanations.3: Energy cost is relevant industrially, but the primary reason is that crude oil has many components with DIFFERENT boiling points that need to be separated simultaneously.

foundation.common_mistake: Filtration only removes INSOLUBLE solids. You CANNOT filter salt from water — salt is dissolved (soluble). Use evaporation or distillation instead. Also: in distillation you collect the VAPOUR that condenses — the liquid. Crystallisation collects the SOLID that forms as the solution cools.

fifas.label: Rf Value Calculation
fifas.question: In a chromatography experiment, a dye spot moves 6.0 cm. The solvent front moves 9.0 cm. Calculate the Rf value.
steps: Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent
No unit conversion needed — Rf has no units

atomic-structure/model-of-the-atom.summary: Describe how the model of the atom developed as new evidence was discovered.

quiz.q: In Rutherford's gold foil experiment, most alpha particles passed straight through the foil. What does this tell us?
opts: Atoms are mostly empty space — most particles found no matter to deflect them
Gold atoms are very large — the particles passed through the gaps between atoms
Alpha particles are too fast to be deflected
The plum pudding model was correct — the positive charge is spread out

wrong_explanations.1: Atomic SIZE explains why particles might miss atoms, not why they pass THROUGH them. The key conclusion is that the atom is mostly empty space internally.
wrong_explanations.2: Alpha particles can be deflected — some WERE deflected at large angles, showing the nucleus exists.
wrong_explanations.3: The plum pudding model predicted only SMALL deflections. The large angle deflections and back-scattering DISPROVED the plum pudding model.

quiz.q: Why did the scientific community accept Rutherford's nuclear model over Thomson's plum pudding model?
opts: The gold foil experiment produced results that could not be explained by the plum pudding model — the new model fitted the evidence
Rutherford was more famous than Thomson, so his model was believed
The plum pudding model was proposed before electrons were discovered
Both models are still equally accepted today

wrong_explanations.1: Scientific acceptance is based on EVIDENCE, not reputation — the gold foil results clearly contradicted the plum pudding model.
wrong_explanations.2: Thomson's plum pudding model was specifically based on and designed to explain the discovery of electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Rutherford's nuclear model replaced the plum pudding model — it is not still equally accepted, though both are discussed historically.

foundation.common_mistake: In Rutherford's experiment, MOST particles passed STRAIGHT THROUGH — this is what shows atoms are mostly empty space. Only a FEW bounced back. Students often get this backwards and say most particles bounced back. The few that did bounce back were what proved the nucleus exists.

atomic-structure/subatomic-particles.summary: Describe the relative charges and masses of protons, neutrons and electrons and the scale of atoms.

quiz.q: A magnesium atom (Mg, atomic number 12) forms a Mg²⁺ ion. How many electrons does the ion have?
opts: 10 — magnesium loses 2 electrons from its original 12
14 — magnesium gains 2 electrons to form a +2 ion
12 — the number of electrons stays the same
2 — only the outermost electrons remain

wrong_explanations.1: Gaining electrons gives a NEGATIVE charge. Mg²⁺ is POSITIVE, so electrons were LOST.
wrong_explanations.2: Mg²⁺ is formed by losing 2 electrons. Original: 12 electrons. After loss: 12 − 2 = 10 electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Only the 2 outermost electrons are lost — but the ion still has all 10 remaining electrons, not just 2.

quiz.q: Why does an atom have no overall electrical charge?
opts: The number of protons (+1 each) equals the number of electrons (−1 each) — the charges cancel
Protons and neutrons have equal and opposite charges that cancel
Electrons are on the outside and shield the positive nucleus
The atom vibrates too fast for any charge to accumulate

wrong_explanations.1: Neutrons have ZERO charge — they cannot cancel proton charges. Charge neutrality comes from protons balancing electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: Shielding describes how inner electrons reduce the attractive force on outer electrons — it does not explain why atoms are electrically neutral.
wrong_explanations.3: Vibration does not affect electrical charge — the neutrality is a consequence of proton and electron numbers being equal.

foundation.common_mistake: The number of PROTONS never changes in a chemical reaction — only electrons are gained or lost to form ions. Also: electrons have negligible mass — almost all atomic mass is in the nucleus (protons + neutrons both have relative mass 1). The atom is MOSTLY EMPTY SPACE — the nucleus is 10,000 times smaller than the atom.

atomic-structure/relative-atomic-mass.summary: Define atomic number, mass number, isotopes and relative atomic mass — and calculate Ar from isotope abundances.

quiz.q: Chlorine-35 and chlorine-37 are isotopes. What is the same and what differs between them?
opts: Same: atomic number (17 protons and 17 electrons). Different: mass number — Cl-35 has 18 neutrons, Cl-37 has 20 neutrons.
Same: mass number. Different: atomic number and proton count.
Same: everything — isotopes are identical atoms.
Same: neutron number. Different: proton number.

wrong_explanations.1: If mass numbers were the same they would be identical — isotopes are DEFINED by having different mass numbers.
wrong_explanations.2: Isotopes DO have the same chemical properties — but they are NOT identical. They differ in neutron count and mass.
wrong_explanations.3: If neutron numbers were the same but proton numbers differed, they would be different ELEMENTS — not isotopes.

quiz.q: An element has two isotopes: 60% at mass 63 and 40% at mass 65. What is its Ar?
opts: 64.0 — simple average of 63 and 65
128 — total of both mass numbers added together
63 — just the most abundant isotope mass

wrong_explanations.1: Simple average gives 64 only if BOTH isotopes are 50/50 abundant. Since there is more of the lighter isotope (60% at mass 63), the weighted average is pulled below 64.
wrong_explanations.2: Adding mass numbers has no physical meaning — Ar is a weighted average, not a sum.
wrong_explanations.3: Using only the most abundant isotope ignores the 40% contribution from mass 65 — the true Ar is pulled above 63 by the heavier isotope.

foundation.common_mistake: Ar is a WEIGHTED average — not a simple average. Chlorine has Ar 35.5 because ~75% is Cl-35 and only ~25% is Cl-37. If both were 50/50, Ar would be 36. The heavier isotope has LESS influence because it's less abundant. Also: isotopes have SAME chemical properties (same electrons) but DIFFERENT physical properties (different mass).

atomic-structure/electronic-structure.summary: Describe how electrons fill shells and write electronic configurations for the first 20 elements.

quiz.q: An element has the electronic configuration 2.8.6. Which group of the periodic table is it in?
opts: Group 6 — it has 6 electrons in its outermost shell
Group 2 — there are 2 complete shells below the outer shell
Group 16 — add all the electrons together
Period 3 — it has 3 shells

wrong_explanations.1: The number of complete inner shells tells you the PERIOD — 2 inner shells means Period 3. Groups are determined by OUTER electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: 16 is the total number of electrons (2+8+6) — this tells you the atomic number, not the group.
wrong_explanations.3: The NUMBER OF SHELLS tells you the PERIOD (3 shells = Period 3). The GROUP is determined by the number of OUTER electrons (6).

quiz.q: Why do lithium (2.1) and sodium (2.8.1) have similar chemical properties?
opts: Both have 1 electron in their outer shell — they react in similar ways to achieve a full outer shell
Both have the same total number of electrons
Both are in the same period of the periodic table
Both have the same mass number

wrong_explanations.1: Li has 3 electrons total, Na has 11 — very different totals, but the same outer shell count (1) is what matters for reactivity.
wrong_explanations.2: Li is Period 2, Na is Period 3 — they are in different periods. Same GROUP, different periods.
wrong_explanations.3: Li has mass number 7, Na has mass number 23 — different mass numbers. Chemical similarity comes from electron configuration.

foundation.common_mistake: Always fill shells in ORDER — 2 in the first, then 8 in the second, then continue. The 3rd shell fills to 8 before the 4th shell starts. Students sometimes try to put more than 8 in the 3rd shell for early elements — for the first 20, the 3rd shell only goes up to 8.

atomic-structure/periodic-table.summary: Describe the structure of the periodic table and explain how it is organised.

quiz.q: An element is in Period 3 and Group 2. What is its electronic configuration?
opts: 2.8.2 — 3 shells (Period 3) and 2 outer electrons (Group 2)
3.2 — period number first, then group number
2.2 — two shells and two outer electrons
2.8.3 — Period 3 means 3 electrons in the outer shell

wrong_explanations.1: The periodic table doesn't give configurations directly — the group number is the outer electrons, but you must fill the inner shells first.
wrong_explanations.2: Period 3 means 3 SHELLS, not 3 electrons in the outer shell. The shells fill as 2.8.2 — inner shells full, outer shell has 2.
wrong_explanations.3: Period 3 means 3 SHELLS — not 3 electrons in the outermost shell. The Group number (2) gives the outer electron count.

quiz.q: Why do all Group 7 elements have similar chemical properties?
opts: They all have 7 electrons in their outer shell — they react similarly by gaining one electron to achieve a full shell
They are all gases at room temperature
They all have the same number of electrons
They are all in the same period of the periodic table

wrong_explanations.1: Fluorine is a gas but iodine is a solid at room temperature — physical state varies within Group 7.
wrong_explanations.2: Different Group 7 elements have different numbers of electrons (F:9, Cl:17, Br:35) — the OUTER electron count (7) is what's the same.
wrong_explanations.3: Elements in the same group are in DIFFERENT periods — they share the same outer electron count, not the same period.

foundation.common_mistake: The GROUP NUMBER tells you the number of OUTER ELECTRONS — not the total electrons or the period. The PERIOD NUMBER tells you how many shells. So an element in Period 3, Group 2 has 3 shells and 2 outer electrons — electronic configuration 2.8.2 (magnesium). Don't confuse groups and periods.

So an element in Period 3, Group 2 has 3 shells and 2 outer electrons — electronic configuration 2.8.2 (magnesium). Don't confuse groups and periods.

Group 7 reactivity decreases down (outer shell further, weaker pull on incoming electron). Predict properties of unknown elements from position.

atomic-structure/development-periodic-ta.summary: Describe how the periodic table developed, including Newlands and Mendeleev's contributions.

quiz.q: Why did the scientific community initially reject Newlands' Law of Octaves?
opts: It only worked for the first 16 elements and forced elements into groups regardless of whether properties matched
Newlands arranged elements by atomic number rather than relative atomic mass
The law of octaves was never published so other scientists couldn't evaluate it
Newlands did not leave gaps for undiscovered elements — but neither did he need to

wrong_explanations.1: Newlands arranged by RELATIVE ATOMIC MASS — atomic numbers were not yet known.
wrong_explanations.2: Newlands did publish his work — it was presented to the Chemical Society in 1866.
wrong_explanations.3: Not leaving gaps was a problem for Mendeleev too initially — but the bigger issue for Newlands was that his pattern broke down after the first 16 elements.

quiz.q: What was the key reason Mendeleev's periodic table was eventually accepted by scientists?
opts: He predicted properties of undiscovered elements — and when they were found, the predictions were accurate
He was the first to arrange elements in order of atomic mass
He discovered all the noble gases and added them to the table
His table was the simplest and easiest to remember

wrong_explanations.1: Newlands had also arranged by atomic mass — Mendeleev's key advance was leaving gaps and making testable predictions.
wrong_explanations.2: Noble gases were discovered by other scientists (Ramsay and others) after Mendeleev's original table — they were later added as Group 0.
wrong_explanations.3: Science accepts models based on EVIDENCE and PREDICTIVE POWER, not simplicity alone.

foundation.common_mistake: Mendeleev arranged elements by RELATIVE ATOMIC MASS — the MODERN table is arranged by ATOMIC NUMBER. Mendeleev's arrangement had a few inconsistencies because Ar and atomic number don't always match perfectly (e.g. argon and potassium). The modern arrangement by atomic number resolves all these problems.

atomic-structure/metals-non-metals.summary: Compare the properties of metals and non-metals and explain them in terms of structure and bonding.

quiz.q: Why are metals good conductors of electricity?
opts: Metals have delocalised (free) electrons that can move through the structure and carry charge
Metal atoms are larger, so electrons move more easily
Metals have more protons than non-metals, creating stronger positive charge
Metal compounds dissolve in water to release ions that carry charge

wrong_explanations.1: Atomic size does not determine conductivity — it is the presence of FREE electrons, not large atoms, that allows charge to flow.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton count determines the element, not conductivity. Non-metals also have many protons but are poor conductors.
wrong_explanations.3: Solid metals conduct electricity — not dissolved ions. Ionic conductivity in solution is different from metallic conductivity in a solid.

quiz.q: Sulfur dioxide (SO₂) dissolves in water to form an acidic solution. Is sulfur a metal or non-metal?
opts: Non-metal — non-metal oxides form acidic solutions when dissolved in water
Metal — metal oxides are always basic
Metalloid — SO₂ is both acidic and basic
It could be either — the acidity of its oxide doesn't indicate the type of element

wrong_explanations.1: Metal oxides are BASIC — they neutralise acids. SO₂ forms an acidic solution, which is the behaviour of NON-METAL oxides.
wrong_explanations.2: SO₂ is not amphoteric (both acidic and basic) — it is distinctly acidic. Sulfur is clearly a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.3: The type of oxide (acidic or basic) is a reliable indicator of whether the element is a metal or non-metal.

foundation.common_mistake: Graphite and graphene are non-metals that conduct electricity — they are exceptions. Most non-metals do not conduct, but graphite has delocalised electrons between its layers. Don't say 'all non-metals don't conduct' — say 'non-metals generally don't conduct, except graphite and graphene'.

atomic-structure/group-0.summary: Describe the properties of noble gases and explain why they are so unreactive.

quiz.q: Why are noble gases so unreactive?
opts: They have full outer electron shells — no tendency to gain, lose or share electrons to become more stable
They are gases — gases cannot react with solid or liquid substances
They have no electrons in their outer shell — so they cannot form bonds
They are too small to collide effectively with other atoms

wrong_explanations.1: Many gases are highly reactive — chlorine gas (Cl₂), oxygen gas (O₂) etc. Being a gas doesn't make something unreactive.
wrong_explanations.2: Noble gases have FULL outer shells, not empty ones. Helium has 2 electrons (full 1st shell), others have 8 outer electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Atomic size affects reactivity in some cases, but noble gases are unreactive because of electron configuration, not size.

quiz.q: Argon has a higher boiling point than neon. Why?
opts: Argon is a larger atom with more electrons — stronger London dispersion forces between atoms need more energy to overcome
Argon has a higher atomic number, so its electrons are more strongly attracted to the nucleus
Argon forms molecules (Ar₂) while neon is monatomic — more bonds to break
Argon is radioactive, releasing energy that raises its boiling point

wrong_explanations.1: Stronger nuclear attraction would make electrons harder to remove but doesn't directly raise boiling point — the intermolecular forces between atoms are what matter.
wrong_explanations.2: Noble gases are ALL monatomic — they never form Ar₂ or Ne₂ molecules under normal conditions.
wrong_explanations.3: Argon is stable and not radioactive. Radon is the radioactive noble gas.

foundation.common_mistake: Noble gases are MONATOMIC — they exist as single atoms (He, Ne, Ar) — NOT as molecules (not He₂ or Ne₂). This is because they are already stable with full outer shells and have no reason to form bonds. Other non-metals like oxygen (O₂) and nitrogen (N₂) exist as diatomic molecules because they share electrons to achieve full outer shells.

atomic-structure/group-1.summary: Describe the properties of Group 1 metals and explain trends in reactivity down the group.

quiz.q: What are the products when sodium reacts with water?
opts: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrogen gas (H₂)
Sodium oxide (Na₂O) and water
Sodium chloride (NaCl) and hydrogen
Sodium hydroxide and oxygen gas

wrong_explanations.1: Sodium reacts with oxygen to form sodium OXIDE — but when it reacts with water specifically, the products are hydroxide and hydrogen.
wrong_explanations.2: NaCl is the product of sodium reacting with CHLORINE or hydrochloric acid — not water.
wrong_explanations.3: Oxygen is not released when metals react with water. The equation is: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂.

quiz.q: Why is potassium more reactive than lithium?
opts: Potassium has more electron shells — its outer electron is further from the nucleus and more easily lost
Potassium has more protons — so it has a stronger positive charge making it more reactive
Potassium is a larger atom so it reacts with a larger surface area
Potassium's outer electron has a higher charge than lithium's outer electron

wrong_explanations.1: More protons means stronger nuclear charge — this would actually make it HARDER to lose the electron, not easier. But the extra shells and increased distance outweigh the effect of more protons.
wrong_explanations.2: Surface area affects reaction rate but not inherent reactivity — the question is about why K is more reactive than Li in terms of properties.
wrong_explanations.3: All electrons have the same charge (−1 each) — the 'ease of losing' them depends on how strongly they are attracted to the nucleus.

foundation.common_mistake: Reactivity INCREASES down Group 1 (opposite to Group 7). This is because the outer electron gets further from the nucleus and is more easily lost. Going down Group 1: more shells → outer electron further away → weaker attraction → MORE reactive. Going down Group 7: non-metals get LESS reactive (harder to attract an extra electron).

atomic-structure/group-7.summary: Describe the properties of halogens and explain trends in reactivity down Group 7.

quiz.q: Chlorine water is added to a solution of potassium bromide. What is observed?
opts: The solution turns orange/brown — chlorine displaces bromide ions, releasing bromine
No change — chlorine cannot react with bromide ions
The solution turns pale green — chlorine is absorbed by the solution
A white precipitate forms — chlorine reacts with potassium ions

wrong_explanations.1: Chlorine IS more reactive than bromine and CAN displace bromide — so a reaction does occur.
wrong_explanations.2: Chlorine water has a yellow-green colour but when it reacts with bromide, the BROWN colour of bromine is what appears in the solution.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine does not react with potassium ions — it displaces bromide ions. No precipitate forms.

quiz.q: Why is fluorine more reactive than chlorine?
opts: Fluorine is a smaller atom — its outer shell is closer to the nucleus, so it attracts an incoming electron more strongly
Fluorine has fewer electrons, making it less stable and more reactive
Fluorine has more protons, making its positive charge stronger
Fluorine is a gas, making it react more easily than other states of matter

wrong_explanations.1: Fewer electrons in an outer shell means the atom needs to GAIN electrons — but that doesn't directly explain why fluorine attracts electrons more strongly. The distance from the nucleus is the key factor.
wrong_explanations.2: Fluorine does have fewer protons (9) than chlorine (17) — but its outer shell is MUCH closer to those protons. The closeness wins over the fewer-proton effect.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine is also a gas — being a gas is not the reason fluorine is more reactive.

quiz.q: Iodine solution is added to sodium chloride solution. What happens?
opts: No reaction — iodine is less reactive than chlorine and cannot displace it
Chlorine gas is released — iodine displaces chloride ions
A brown precipitate forms — iodine reacts with sodium ions
The solution turns pale yellow — iodine bleaches the solution

wrong_explanations.1: A more reactive halogen displaces a less reactive one. Iodine is LESS reactive than chlorine — so iodine cannot displace chloride ions.
wrong_explanations.2: Iodine does not react with sodium ions — and even if it tried to displace chloride, it couldn't because iodine is less reactive than chlorine.
wrong_explanations.3: Iodine actually turns starch blue-black — it doesn't bleach. Chlorine is the halogen with bleaching properties.

foundation.common_mistake: Reactivity DECREASES down Group 7 — the OPPOSITE of Group 1. In Group 1, going down makes metals MORE reactive (outer electron easier to LOSE). In Group 7, going down makes non-metals LESS reactive (harder to GAIN an extra electron when the outer shell is further away).

atomic-structure/transition-metals.summary: Compare transition metals with Group 1 metals and describe their typical properties and uses.

quiz.q: Why are transition metals more useful as structural materials than Group 1 metals?
opts: Transition metals have high melting points, high density and high strength — Group 1 metals are soft, low-density and too reactive for structural use
Transition metals are cheaper than Group 1 metals — cost makes them more practical
Group 1 metals are radioactive — too dangerous for use in buildings
Transition metals are lighter than Group 1 metals — easier to work with structurally

wrong_explanations.1: Cost is a practical factor but not the reason given in chemistry. The key reasons are mechanical properties: strength, hardness, melting point.
wrong_explanations.2: Group 1 metals (lithium, sodium, potassium etc.) are NOT radioactive — they are just very reactive with water and oxygen, making them unsuitable for structural use.
wrong_explanations.3: Transition metals are actually DENSER (heavier) than Group 1 metals — the advantages are strength and chemical stability, not lower weight.

quiz.q: Which transition metal is used as a catalyst in the Haber process to manufacture ammonia?
opts: Iron — it acts as a catalyst to speed up the reaction between nitrogen and hydrogen
Copper — widely used catalyst in many industrial processes
Platinum — the most effective transition metal catalyst
Nickel — used in all industrial catalytic processes

wrong_explanations.1: Copper is a good conductor but not the catalyst used in the Haber process.
wrong_explanations.2: Platinum is used in catalytic converters for car exhaust and in the Contact process — not the Haber process.
wrong_explanations.3: Nickel is used in hydrogenation of vegetable oils — not the Haber process.

foundation.common_mistake: Transition metals have HIGH melting points, HIGH density and are HARD — the opposite of Group 1. Group 1 are soft, low melting point, low density and very reactive. Don't confuse their properties. Mercury is the exception among transition metals — it is a liquid at room temperature.
foundation.triple_box: Properties of transition metals (4.1.3) is chemistry-only — not in Combined Science. Covers comparison with Group 1 and characteristic properties: coloured compounds, catalytic behaviour, variable oxidation states, and high melting points/density.

bonding/chemical-bonds.summary: Describe why atoms form chemical bonds and the three main types of bonding.

quiz.q: Which type of bonding occurs between a metal and a non-metal?
opts: Ionic bonding — the metal transfers electrons to the non-metal, forming oppositely charged ions
Covalent bonding — the metal shares electrons with the non-metal
Metallic bonding — electrons are released into a delocalised sea
Hydrogen bonding — a weak attraction between the two atoms

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonding occurs between NON-METALS only — metals do not typically share electrons.
wrong_explanations.2: Metallic bonding occurs in PURE METALS or alloys — not between a metal and a non-metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Hydrogen bonding is a type of intermolecular force, not a primary bond type between elements — and it is not what forms between a metal and non-metal.

quiz.q: Magnesium (Group 2) reacts with oxygen (Group 6) to form magnesium oxide. What type of bonding does MgO have?
opts: Ionic — magnesium transfers 2 electrons to oxygen, forming Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ ions
Covalent — they share 2 electrons to fill their outer shells
Metallic — magnesium releases electrons into a sea
No bonding — they don't react because both are stable elements

wrong_explanations.1: Magnesium and oxygen share electrons in covalent bonds — this only happens between two non-metals. Magnesium is a metal.
wrong_explanations.2: Metallic bonding occurs in metals only — not in compounds containing both a metal and non-metal.
wrong_explanations.3: Magnesium burns vigorously in oxygen — it is very reactive. The product MgO is a stable ionic compound.

foundation.common_mistake: The strong bond in a covalent MOLECULE is between atoms within the molecule. The WEAK forces between separate molecules (intermolecular forces) are what give simple molecular substances their LOW melting points. Students often confuse the strong covalent bond (within a molecule) with the weak intermolecular forces (between molecules).

bonding/ionic-bonding.summary: Describe how ionic bonds form by electron transfer between metals and non-metals.

quiz.q: When sodium (2.8.1) reacts with chlorine (2.8.7) to form NaCl, what happens?
opts: Sodium loses 1 electron → Na⁺ (2.8). Chlorine gains 1 electron → Cl⁻ (2.8.8). Opposite charges attract.
Sodium and chlorine share 1 electron pair to form a single covalent bond.
Both atoms lose electrons to form a positive lattice structure.
Chlorine loses 1 electron to sodium, making chlorine positive.

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonds involve sharing — NaCl is ionic (metal + non-metal = electron TRANSFER).
wrong_explanations.2: In ionic bonding only the METAL loses electrons — the non-metal GAINS them.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine GAINS electrons (it needs 1 more to fill its outer shell) — it doesn't lose them. Non-metals gain electrons to form negative ions.

quiz.q: What is the formula of calcium chloride? (Calcium is Group 2, Chlorine is Group 7)
opts: CaCl₂ — Ca forms Ca²⁺, each Cl forms Cl⁻, so 2 chloride ions needed to balance the 2+ charge
CaCl — 1 calcium bonds with 1 chlorine
Ca₂Cl — 2 calcium atoms bond with 1 chlorine
CaCl₃ — calcium needs 3 chlorine atoms to be stable

wrong_explanations.1: CaCl would give a total charge of +2 + (−1) = +1 — not neutral. Ionic compounds must have zero overall charge.
wrong_explanations.2: Ca₂Cl would give 2×(+2) + (−1) = +3 — not neutral.
wrong_explanations.3: CaCl₃ would give +2 + 3×(−1) = −1 — not neutral. You need exactly 2 Cl⁻ to balance 1 Ca²⁺.

foundation.common_mistake: The IONIC BOND is the electrostatic attraction between OPPOSITELY charged ions — NOT the transfer of electrons itself. The transfer creates the ions; the attraction between them IS the bond. Also: ionic compounds always have an overall charge of ZERO — the positive and negative charges must balance.

quiz.q: When sodium (2.8.1) reacts with chlorine (2.8.7) to form NaCl, what happens?
opts: Sodium loses 1 electron → Na⁺ (2.8). Chlorine gains 1 electron → Cl⁻ (2.8.8). Opposite charges attract.
Sodium and chlorine share 1 electron pair to form a single covalent bond.
Both atoms lose electrons to form a positive lattice structure.
Chlorine loses 1 electron to sodium, making chlorine positive.

wrong_explanations.1: Covalent bonds involve sharing — NaCl is ionic (metal + non-metal = electron TRANSFER).
wrong_explanations.2: In ionic bonding only the METAL loses electrons — the non-metal GAINS them.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorine GAINS electrons (it needs 1 more to fill its outer shell) — it doesn't lose them. Non-metals gain electrons to form negative ions.

quiz.q: What is the formula of calcium chloride? (Calcium is Group 2, Chlorine is Group 7)
opts: CaCl₂ — Ca forms Ca²⁺, each Cl forms Cl⁻, so 2 chloride ions needed to balance the 2+ charge
CaCl — 1 calcium bonds with 1 chlorine
Ca₂Cl — 2 calcium atoms bond with 1 chlorine
CaCl₃ — calcium needs 3 chlorine atoms to be stable

wrong_explanations.1: CaCl would give a total charge of +2 + (−1) = +1 — not neutral. Ionic compounds must have zero overall charge.
wrong_explanations.2: Ca₂Cl would give 2×(+2) + (−1) = +3 — not neutral.
wrong_explanations.3: CaCl₃ would give +2 + 3×(−1) = −1 — not neutral. You need exactly 2 Cl⁻ to balance 1 Ca²⁺.

The transfer creates the ions; the attraction between them IS the bond. Also: ionic compounds always have an overall charge of ZERO — the positive and negative charges must balance.

bonding/ionic-compounds.summary: Describe the giant ionic lattice structure of ionic compounds and link it to their properties.

quiz.q: Why does solid sodium chloride not conduct electricity, but molten sodium chloride does?
opts: Solid: ions in fixed positions — cannot move. Molten: ions free to move and carry charge.
Solid NaCl has no ions — they only form when the compound melts.
Solid NaCl is an insulator because it is white — colour prevents conductivity.
Molten NaCl produces electrons that carry the current — not ions.

wrong_explanations.1: NaCl is always ionic — the Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions exist in the solid lattice. They just can't MOVE when solid.
wrong_explanations.2: Colour has no effect on electrical conductivity.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic conduction is carried by IONS (charged atoms), not electrons. Electron conduction occurs in metals.

quiz.q: Why do ionic compounds have high melting points?
opts: Strong electrostatic forces between many oppositely charged ions throughout the giant lattice require a lot of energy to overcome
The large size of ionic crystals means more energy is needed to melt the whole crystal
Ionic compounds contain strong covalent bonds that must be broken
The electrons in ionic compounds are tightly held and resist heating

wrong_explanations.1: Physical size of a crystal doesn't determine melting point — the STRENGTH of bonding does.
wrong_explanations.2: Ionic compounds contain IONIC bonds (electrostatic attractions between ions) — not covalent bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: Electrons in ionic compounds are transferred to ions — they are not the source of high melting point.

foundation.common_mistake: Ionic compounds conduct electricity when MOLTEN or in SOLUTION — NOT when solid. The ions in solid ionic compounds are held in fixed positions in the lattice and cannot move. Melting or dissolving gives the ions freedom to move — and moving charged particles = electrical conductivity.

bonding/covalent-bonding.summary: Describe how covalent bonds form by electron sharing between non-metal atoms.

quiz.q: Carbon (Group 4) bonds with hydrogen (Group 1) to form methane (CH₄). How many covalent bonds does carbon form?
opts: 4 — carbon has 4 outer electrons and needs 4 more to achieve a full shell of 8
1 — carbon only forms one bond at a time
2 — carbon forms 2 bonds like oxygen
8 — carbon needs to reach a total of 8 electrons

wrong_explanations.1: Carbon forms one bond per atom, not one bond total. Each covalent bond shares 2 electrons — carbon forms 4 bonds to share 4 × 2 = 8 electrons total.
wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen (Group 6) needs 2 more electrons — so it forms 2 bonds. Carbon (Group 4) needs 4 more.
wrong_explanations.3: Carbon needs 8 electrons in its outer shell — but each BOND provides 2 shared electrons. 4 bonds × 2 = 8 electrons total in the outer shell.

quiz.q: Why does oxygen exist as O₂ rather than individual O atoms?
opts: An oxygen atom has 6 outer electrons — sharing 2 pairs with another O atom gives both atoms a full outer shell of 8
Two oxygen atoms bond to make a more stable element in the periodic table
Oxygen atoms repel each other and form O₂ to cancel the repulsion
O₂ is formed because oxygen needs to be a gas at room temperature

wrong_explanations.1: This confuses bonding with stability classification — O₂ forms because of electron configuration, not because it changes the element's position.
wrong_explanations.2: Atoms of the same element have the same charge and actually do repel each other slightly — but covalent bonding (SHARING electrons) overcomes this by creating a stronger attraction.
wrong_explanations.3: Whether a substance is a gas depends on intermolecular forces, not on why O₂ forms. O₂ forms because of electronic structure.

foundation.common_mistake: Covalent bonds are STRONG — but simple molecular substances have LOW melting points because the FORCES BETWEEN molecules (intermolecular forces) are WEAK. The covalent bond is within the molecule; melting involves separating molecules from each other (overcoming intermolecular forces), not breaking the bonds within them.

higher.higher_box: Draw dot-and-cross diagrams for: H₂, Cl₂, HCl, O₂ (double bond), N₂ (triple bond), CH₄, H₂O, NH₃, CO₂. Show lone pairs on relevant atoms. Understand bond polarity in terms of electronegativity differences. The shared electrons are attracted to both nuclei — electrostatic attraction is the covalent bond.

bonding/metallic-bonding.summary: Describe the metallic bonding model and use it to explain the properties of metals.

quiz.q: Why are metals good conductors of electricity?
opts: Delocalised electrons can move freely through the metallic lattice and carry charge
Metal ions are positively charged and attract electrical current
Metals have more protons than non-metals, making them better at conducting
The regular lattice of metal ions allows electrical signals to travel between them

wrong_explanations.1: Positive ions ATTRACT electrons but do not themselves move to carry current — it is the mobile delocalised electrons that carry charge.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton count determines the element's identity, not its conductivity — non-metals can also have many protons but are poor conductors.
wrong_explanations.3: The lattice provides the structure, but conductivity comes from the FREE electrons, not from the ions themselves.

quiz.q: Why is steel harder than pure iron?
opts: Carbon atoms in steel have a different size to iron atoms — they disrupt the regular lattice and prevent layers from sliding
Steel has stronger metallic bonds than iron because it contains more electrons
Carbon atoms fill the gaps between iron atoms, increasing density and hardness
Steel is harder because it has been purified — removing impurities strengthens the metal

wrong_explanations.1: Steel has more metallic bonds in total, but the KEY reason is DISRUPTION of the lattice by differently-sized atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Carbon atoms do fill interstitial spaces, but the hardening mechanism is specifically about PREVENTING LAYER SLIDING — the lattice disruption model.
wrong_explanations.3: Steel is iron made LESS pure (with carbon added) — it is the ADDITION of a different-sized element that makes it harder, not purification.

foundation.common_mistake: The metallic bond is the attraction between POSITIVE METAL IONS and the SEA OF DELOCALISED ELECTRONS — not between individual atoms directly. Malleability works because ion LAYERS slide — the electron sea allows this without breaking bonds. If layers had to break ionic bonds (like in ionic compounds), the metal would shatter instead of bend.

bonding/states-of-matter.summary: Describe the three states of matter, the changes between them and how to use state symbols.

quiz.q: What state symbol is used for a substance dissolved in water?
opts: (aq) — aqueous, meaning dissolved in water
(l) — liquid, because a solution is a liquid
(d) — dissolved
(w) — water solution

wrong_explanations.1: (l) means PURE liquid — e.g. liquid water H₂O(l). A solution of NaCl in water would use NaCl(aq), not NaCl(l).
wrong_explanations.2: There is no (d) state symbol in chemistry equations.
wrong_explanations.3: The four state symbols are (s), (l), (g) and (aq) — no (w) symbol exists.

quiz.q: Ice melts to form water. Is this a physical or chemical change? Explain.
opts: Physical change — the chemical formula stays as H₂O; only the arrangement of particles changes
Chemical change — a new substance (water) is produced from ice
Chemical change — energy is added, which must have broken chemical bonds
Neither — melting is a change of state, not classified as physical or chemical

wrong_explanations.1: Ice and water are the same substance — H₂O. Water is NOT a new substance; it is just the same H₂O molecules in a different state.
wrong_explanations.2: Energy can be added without breaking covalent bonds — melting ice only overcomes the INTERMOLECULAR FORCES (hydrogen bonds between water molecules), not the covalent O-H bonds.
wrong_explanations.3: Changes of state ARE classified as physical changes — the composition of the substance does not change.

foundation.common_mistake: Changes of state are PHYSICAL changes — the substance does not change chemically. H₂O is always H₂O whether it is ice, water or steam. (aq) means dissolved in WATER specifically — not just any liquid. A substance in ethanol or acetone would NOT be labelled (aq).

bonding/properties-ionic-compounds.summary: Explain the properties of ionic compounds in terms of their giant lattice structure.

quiz.q: Magnesium oxide (MgO) has a higher melting point than sodium chloride (NaCl). Why?
opts: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ have higher charges (±2) than Na⁺ and Cl⁻ (±1) — stronger electrostatic forces between ions
MgO has a larger crystal lattice, so more bonds must be broken
MgO contains more ions per formula unit than NaCl
Magnesium is a heavier element than sodium — this increases the melting point

wrong_explanations.1: Crystal size is not the determining factor — both have giant lattices. The STRENGTH of individual ion-ion attractions matters.
wrong_explanations.2: Both NaCl and MgO have a 1:1 ratio of ions in their formula — neither has more ions per formula unit.
wrong_explanations.3: Atomic mass does not determine melting point — the CHARGE on the ions determines the strength of electrostatic forces.

quiz.q: A student tests whether NaCl conducts electricity in four forms: solid, dissolved in water, molten, and as a gas. In which forms does it conduct?
opts: Dissolved in water and molten — ions are free to move in both cases
Only in solid form — the lattice provides a pathway for electrons
In all forms — NaCl always has ions present that can carry charge
Only when dissolved — the water provides the conducting pathway

wrong_explanations.1: Solid NaCl has ions but they are FIXED — they cannot move to carry charge.
wrong_explanations.2: Ions present does NOT mean they can conduct — they must be FREE TO MOVE. In a solid, they are fixed in the lattice.
wrong_explanations.3: Molten NaCl also conducts — water is not needed. Free ions (whether from melting or dissolving) allow conduction.

foundation.common_mistake: Ionic compounds conduct in solution because the IONS move — NOT because electrons move. This is IONIC conduction (different from ELECTRONIC conduction in metals). Also: ionic compounds are brittle, not ductile. They crack when struck because shifting layers brings like charges together → repulsion → shattering.

bonding/properties-small-molecules.summary: Explain why simple molecular substances have low melting points and do not conduct electricity.

quiz.q: Water (H₂O) boils at 100°C, but methane (CH₄) boils at −161°C. Both are simple molecular substances. Why is the difference so large?
opts: Water molecules form stronger intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) than methane — more energy needed to separate water molecules
Water has more covalent bonds — it takes more energy to break them
Methane is lighter and therefore evaporates faster
Water contains oxygen, which makes molecules heavier and harder to separate

wrong_explanations.1: Boiling does NOT break covalent bonds — water has 2 covalent bonds, methane has 4. Bond count is not the reason.
wrong_explanations.2: Lighter molecules do evaporate faster at a given temperature, but boiling point is about the temperature at which intermolecular forces are overcome — not just molecular mass.
wrong_explanations.3: Oxygen does make water heavier, but the primary reason is STRONGER INTERMOLECULAR FORCES (hydrogen bonds) in water compared to the weaker London dispersion forces in methane.

quiz.q: Why do simple molecular substances not conduct electricity?
opts: They have no free electrons or ions — there are no charged particles that can move to carry a current
The covalent bonds are too strong to allow electron movement
The molecules are too small to carry a useful electrical charge
Simple molecules are always gases, and gases don't conduct electricity

wrong_explanations.1: Electrical conduction depends on FREE MOVING charged particles — not on bond strength. Even if bonds were weaker, without free charges there would be no conductivity.
wrong_explanations.2: Molecular size doesn't determine conductivity — metals are much larger structures but conduct excellently because of free electrons.
wrong_explanations.3: Some gases DO conduct electricity (e.g. plasma, ionised gas) — and simple molecular substances can be solids or liquids too.

foundation.common_mistake: Simple molecular substances have LOW melting points because INTERMOLECULAR FORCES between molecules are weak — NOT because covalent bonds are weak. The covalent bonds WITHIN molecules are actually very strong. The confusion between these two types of force is one of the most common errors in this topic.

bonding/polymers.summary: Describe the structure of polymers and explain their properties.

quiz.q: What is the monomer used to make poly(ethene)?
opts: Ethene — CH₂=CH₂ — the double bond opens up and monomers join together
Ethane — C₂H₆ — a saturated hydrocarbon that joins together
Ethanol — C₂H₅OH — an alcohol that polymerises
Methane — CH₄ — the simplest hydrocarbon

wrong_explanations.1: Ethane is SATURATED (no double bond) — addition polymerisation requires a C=C double bond. Ethane cannot polymerise by addition.
wrong_explanations.2: Ethanol can undergo condensation reactions but does not form poly(ethene) — and poly(ethene) is produced by addition polymerisation.
wrong_explanations.3: Methane has no double bond and only 1 carbon — it cannot chain together to form poly(ethene).

quiz.q: Why do polymers have lower melting points than giant covalent structures like diamond?
opts: Polymers are separate large molecules — held together by weak intermolecular forces. Diamond has strong covalent bonds throughout the entire structure.
Polymers contain fewer atoms than diamond — less matter to heat up
Diamond has ionic bonds which are stronger than covalent bonds in polymers
Polymers are synthetic — natural structures like diamond are always stronger

wrong_explanations.1: Polymers are LARGE molecules — a single polymer chain may contain millions of atoms. The issue is the TYPE of forces between chains, not the number of atoms.
wrong_explanations.2: Diamond is entirely COVALENT — it has no ionic bonds. The covalent bonds extending throughout the 3D diamond structure are what make it so hard and high-melting.
wrong_explanations.3: Both natural and synthetic structures can have high or low melting points — it depends on structure and bonding, not whether they are naturally occurring.

foundation.common_mistake: Polymers are LARGE MOLECULES — they are NOT giant covalent structures. Each polymer chain is one large molecule held to others by intermolecular forces (which are weak). Giant covalent structures (like diamond) have covalent bonds extending throughout the entire structure — very different. This is why polymers have much lower melting points than diamond.

bonding/giant-covalent-structures.summary: Describe the structures of diamond and graphite and explain their contrasting properties.

quiz.q: Why does graphite conduct electricity but diamond does not?
opts: Graphite has one delocalised electron per carbon atom — free to move and carry charge. Diamond uses all 4 outer electrons in bonds — none are free.
Graphite contains more carbon atoms than diamond — more atoms means better conductivity
Diamond is transparent — light passes through, but electricity cannot follow light
Graphite has metallic bonding while diamond has ionic bonding

wrong_explanations.1: The NUMBER of carbon atoms doesn't determine conductivity — it is the availability of FREE electrons that matters.
wrong_explanations.2: Transparency and electrical conductivity are completely unrelated properties.
wrong_explanations.3: Both diamond and graphite are entirely COVALENT — neither has metallic or ionic bonding.

quiz.q: Why is graphite used as a lubricant?
opts: Graphite has layers of carbon atoms held by weak intermolecular forces — the layers can slide over each other easily
Graphite is smooth because it has no atoms on its surface
Graphite dissolves in water, creating a slippery solution
Graphite is very hard like diamond, which makes surfaces it coats more slippery

wrong_explanations.1: Surfaces always have atoms — graphite is slippery because of its LAYERED STRUCTURE where layers slide.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphite does not dissolve in water — it is used as a dry lubricant in situations where oil would be problematic.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond is hard but NOT slippery — hardness and slipperiness are different properties. Graphite is SOFT, not hard.

foundation.common_mistake: GRAPHITE conducts electricity — it is the exception to the rule that giant covalent structures don't conduct. Diamond does NOT conduct. The difference: in graphite, each carbon uses only 3 of its 4 outer electrons for bonds — the 4th electron is delocalised and free to move. In diamond, all 4 electrons are used in bonds — none are free.

quiz.q: Why does graphite conduct electricity but diamond does not?
opts: Graphite has one delocalised electron per carbon atom — free to move and carry charge. Diamond uses all 4 outer electrons in bonds — none are free.
Graphite contains more carbon atoms than diamond — more atoms means better conductivity
Diamond is transparent — light passes through, but electricity cannot follow light
Graphite has metallic bonding while diamond has ionic bonding

wrong_explanations.1: The NUMBER of carbon atoms doesn't determine conductivity — it is the availability of FREE electrons that matters.
wrong_explanations.2: Transparency and electrical conductivity are completely unrelated properties.
wrong_explanations.3: Both diamond and graphite are entirely COVALENT — neither has metallic or ionic bonding.

quiz.q: Why is graphite used as a lubricant?
opts: Graphite has layers of carbon atoms held by weak intermolecular forces — the layers can slide over each other easily
Graphite is smooth because it has no atoms on its surface
Graphite dissolves in water, creating a slippery solution
Graphite is very hard like diamond, which makes surfaces it coats more slippery

wrong_explanations.1: Surfaces always have atoms — graphite is slippery because of its LAYERED STRUCTURE where layers slide.
wrong_explanations.2: Graphite does not dissolve in water — it is used as a dry lubricant in situations where oil would be problematic.
wrong_explanations.3: Diamond is hard but NOT slippery — hardness and slipperiness are different properties. Graphite is SOFT, not hard.

The difference: in graphite, each carbon uses only 3 of its 4 outer electrons for bonds — the 4th electron is delocalised and free to move. In diamond, all 4 electrons are used in bonds — none are free.
higher.higher_box: Graphene: a single layer of graphite — one atom thick, conducts electricity (delocalised electrons), extremely strong. Fullerenes: C₆₀ (buckminsterfullerene) — hollow spherical cage, used in drug delivery and as lubricant. Carbon nanotubes: cylindrical graphene — very strong along axis, conduct electricity. All based on hexagonal carbon networks.

bonding/metals-alloys.summary: Explain the properties of metals and alloys in terms of metallic bonding and structure.

quiz.q: Why can metals conduct electricity in the solid state, but ionic compounds cannot?
opts: Metals have delocalised electrons that are free to move even when solid. Ionic solids have ions fixed in the lattice — they cannot move.
Metal atoms are smaller than ions, so they move more easily through the solid
Metals have more protons, making them better at conducting positive charge
Ionic compounds have stronger bonds that prevent any particle movement

wrong_explanations.1: Atomic size doesn't determine conductivity — it's the AVAILABILITY OF FREE CHARGE CARRIERS that matters.
wrong_explanations.2: Proton count doesn't determine conductivity — non-metals can have many protons but are poor conductors.
wrong_explanations.3: Ionic bonds are strong — but ionic compounds CAN conduct when MELTED (ions become free). The issue in solids is that ions are FIXED, not that bonds are stronger.

quiz.q: Why is brass (copper + zinc) harder than pure copper?
opts: Zinc atoms are a different size to copper atoms — they disrupt the regular lattice and prevent layers from sliding
Brass has more metallic bonds than copper — more bonds means harder
Zinc is a harder metal than copper, so mixing them gives a harder product
Brass has a different crystal structure that is always harder than face-centred cubic

wrong_explanations.1: More bonds doesn't necessarily mean harder — the mechanism is LATTICE DISRUPTION preventing layer sliding.
wrong_explanations.2: Zinc alone is harder than pure copper, but the hardening in brass comes specifically from DISRUPTING the regular copper lattice with differently-sized zinc atoms.
wrong_explanations.3: Crystal structure does change in alloys — but the explanation at GCSE level focuses on differently-sized atoms disrupting lattice planes.

This is because metallic conduction uses DELOCALISED ELECTRONS (which are already free to move in the solid), whereas ionic conduction requires ions to move (which they can't do when fixed in a solid lattice).

bonding/nanoparticles.summary: Describe nanoparticles and fullerenes, their properties and uses, and potential risks.

quiz.q: Why do nanoparticles have different properties from the same material in bulk form?
opts: Nanoparticles have a much larger surface area to volume ratio — a greater proportion of atoms are on the surface and available for reactions
Nanoparticles are made of different atoms than bulk materials
Nanoparticles have weaker chemical bonds than bulk materials
Nanoparticles are always more stable than bulk materials

wrong_explanations.1: Nanoparticles ARE made of the same atoms — gold nanoparticles contain gold atoms, just as bulk gold does. It is the SCALE that changes properties.
wrong_explanations.2: The chemical bonds in nanoparticles are the same type as in bulk materials — it is the fraction of surface atoms that differs.
wrong_explanations.3: Nanoparticles are often MORE reactive than bulk materials — which can mean LESS stable in some contexts. Their behaviour is more complex than 'always more stable'.

quiz.q: Why might nanoparticles pose a greater health risk than the same material in larger particle form?
opts: Their small size allows them to penetrate cell membranes, enter cells and potentially cause biological damage — and their long-term effects are not yet fully understood
Nanoparticles are more toxic because they contain more of the harmful material
Nanoparticles are heavier and stay in the lungs longer than larger particles
Nanoparticles react with water in the body to produce dangerous chemicals

wrong_explanations.1: Nanoparticles contain LESS material than larger particles of the same substance — but their small size and high reactivity are the concern, not quantity.
wrong_explanations.2: Nanoparticles are actually LIGHTER than larger particles — the concern is their small size allowing penetration into biological systems.
wrong_explanations.3: Some nanoparticles may react with biological molecules — but the primary concern is their ability to penetrate cell membranes due to their tiny size.

foundation.common_mistake: Nanoparticles are NOT the same as atoms or small molecules — they contain hundreds to thousands of atoms. The key reason nanoparticles have different properties is their enormous SURFACE AREA TO VOLUME RATIO — a much greater fraction of their atoms are on the surface, making them much more reactive than the same material in bulk form.

Evaluate uses: TiO₂ in sunscreen (transparent at nanoscale, blocks UV), silver nanoparticles (antibacterial). quantitative/conservation-of-mass.summary: Explain the law of conservation of mass and balance symbol equations.

quiz.q: Why must chemical equations be balanced?
opts: Atoms cannot be created or destroyed — the same atoms must appear on both sides of the equation
Balanced equations are easier to read and understand
Unbalanced equations produce different products
Equations must be balanced to follow international chemical naming rules

wrong_explanations.1: Readability is a benefit but not the fundamental reason — conservation of mass is.
wrong_explanations.2: An unbalanced equation can still show the correct reactants and products — it just doesn't correctly represent the quantitative relationship between them.
wrong_explanations.3: Balancing is a scientific law requirement (conservation of mass), not a naming convention.

quiz.q: 24 g of magnesium reacts completely with oxygen to form magnesium oxide. 40 g of magnesium oxide is produced. How much oxygen reacted?
opts: 16 g — conservation of mass: mass of O₂ = 40 − 24 = 16 g
40 g — the oxygen has the same mass as the product
64 g — double the mass of magnesium
Cannot be determined without the balanced equation

wrong_explanations.1: Mass in = mass out. If 40 g MgO is made from 24 g Mg, the remaining 40 − 24 = 16 g must have come from oxygen.
wrong_explanations.2: The mass of oxygen is only part of the product mass — the product contains both magnesium AND oxygen.
wrong_explanations.3: Conservation of mass allows this calculation: mass O₂ = mass product − mass Mg = 40 − 24 = 16 g.

quiz.q: Which of these is a correctly balanced equation?
opts: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃
Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃
2Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃
Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃

wrong_explanations.1: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃: left has 1 Fe, 2 O; right has 2 Fe, 3 O — not balanced.
wrong_explanations.2: 2Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃: left has 2 Fe, 2 O; right has 2 Fe, 3 O — oxygen not balanced.
wrong_explanations.3: Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃: left has 1 Fe, 6 O; right has 4 Fe, 6 O — iron not balanced.

foundation.common_mistake: Never change the SUBSCRIPT numbers in a formula when balancing — only add or change the large numbers (coefficients) IN FRONT of formulas. Changing H₂O to H₂O₂ would create a different compound (hydrogen peroxide, not water). Always balance by adjusting coefficients only.

higher.higher_box: Calculate theoretical yield using moles. Atom economy = (Mr desired products ÷ Mr all products) × 100. Addition reactions have 100% atom economy. High atom economy = less waste, more sustainable, more economical. Evaluate different reaction routes on the basis of atom economy.

quantitative/relative-formula-mass.summary: Calculate relative formula mass (Mr) from relative atomic masses and use it in calculations.

quiz.q: What is the relative formula mass of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)? Ar: Ca=40, C=12, O=16.
opts: 68 — (40) + (12) + (16) — counting only one oxygen
116 — (40) + (12) + (4 × 16) — counting four oxygens
52 — (40) + (12) — forgetting the oxygens

wrong_explanations.1: CaCO₃ has only ONE oxygen — but the subscript 3 after O means THREE oxygens: 3 × 16 = 48.
wrong_explanations.2: CO₃ means 3 oxygen atoms, not 4. Only CO₄ would have 4 oxygens.
wrong_explanations.3: The oxygen cannot be ignored — CaCO₃ has a carbon AND three oxygens.

quiz.q: What is the Mr of Mg(NO₃)₂? Ar: Mg=24, N=14, O=16.

wrong_explanations.1: The subscript 2 outside the bracket means the entire NO₃ group is repeated twice. So there is 1 N, NOT 2 — wait: NO₃ has 1 N and 3 O, multiplied by 2 = 2N and 6O.
wrong_explanations.2: Mg(NO₃)₂ means 2 groups of NO₃: so 2N and 6O total. Only counting 3O ignores the ×2 multiplier.
wrong_explanations.3: Each NO₃ has 3 O; multiplied by 2 = 6 O total, not 4.

foundation.common_mistake: When a formula has BRACKETS with a subscript, MULTIPLY all atoms inside the bracket by the subscript. Ca(OH)₂ has 2 oxygen atoms and 2 hydrogen atoms — not 1 of each. Write out the count carefully: Ca(OH)₂ = Ca + 2O + 2H = 40 + 32 + 2 = 74.

fifas.label: Mr Calculation — H₂SO₄
fifas.question: Calculate the relative formula mass of sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄). Ar: H=1, S=32, O=16.
steps: Mr = sum of all Ar values × number of atoms

higher.higher_box: Calculate percentage mass of an element in a compound: (Ar × number of atoms ÷ Mr) × 100. Calculate empirical formula from percentage composition. Convert empirical to molecular formula using Mr. These skills are foundations for mole calculations.

quantitative/mass-changes-reactions.summary: Explain why the measured mass sometimes appears to change in a reaction and predict mass changes.

quiz.q: A student heats calcium carbonate in an open crucible. CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. What happens to the measured mass?
opts: The mass decreases — CO₂ gas escapes from the open crucible
The mass increases — oxygen from air is absorbed
The mass stays the same — conservation of mass
The mass first increases, then decreases

wrong_explanations.1: Oxygen is not absorbed here — CaCO₃ is decomposing, not combusting. A gas (CO₂) is PRODUCED and escapes.
wrong_explanations.2: Conservation of mass applies to the TOTAL system. In an open container, the mass of the CONTAINER contents decreases because CO₂ leaves. Conservation is still upheld if you include the escaped CO₂.
wrong_explanations.3: There is no initial increase phase — CO₂ is produced and escapes from the start of heating.

quiz.q: Why does the mass of iron increase when it rusts in air?
opts: Oxygen from the air combines with the iron — the oxygen adds to the mass of the solid product (iron oxide)
Water from humidity condenses on the rust — adding extra mass
The iron becomes denser as it rusts — same volume but more mass
Iron oxide has more atoms per formula unit than iron

wrong_explanations.1: Humidity can contribute, but the primary reason is oxygen being ABSORBED from the air. The reaction is 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃ — the oxygen becomes part of the solid.
wrong_explanations.2: Density changes do not increase total mass — mass = density × volume. If both change proportionally, mass stays the same. More mass comes from more matter (oxygen absorbed).
wrong_explanations.3: Atom count per formula unit is irrelevant — it is the MASS of oxygen absorbed from air that increases the total mass.

foundation.common_mistake: When a metal burns in air, the solid GAINS mass (oxygen is added from the air to form the oxide). Students often expect the solid to lose mass because 'burning destroys things' — but mass is always conserved. The apparent gain is real because you are adding oxygen from the atmosphere to the solid product.

fifas.label: Mass Change Prediction
fifas.question: 4.8 g of magnesium burns completely in air: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Calculate the mass of MgO produced. Ar: Mg=24, O=16.
steps: Use ratio from balanced equation: 2 × Mr(Mg) : 2 × Mr(MgO) = 48 : 80
Scale: 4.8 g of Mg. Scale factor = 4.8 ÷ 48 = 0.1
8.0 g of MgO produced (mass increases by 3.2 g — the absorbed oxygen)

quantitative/chemical-measurements.summary: Describe the importance of precise measurements in chemistry and sources of uncertainty.

quiz.q: A student measures a volume using a burette and a measuring cylinder. The burette has an uncertainty of ±0.05 cm³ and the cylinder ±1 cm³. Both deliver 25 cm³. Which gives the lower percentage uncertainty?
opts: The burette — % uncertainty = (0.05 ÷ 25) × 100 = 0.2%, vs the cylinder at 4%
The measuring cylinder — larger equipment is always more accurate
They are the same — both deliver 25 cm³
The measuring cylinder — it has a smaller absolute uncertainty

wrong_explanations.1: Equipment size doesn't determine accuracy — precision depends on the uncertainty relative to the measurement.
wrong_explanations.2: Both deliver the same VOLUME but with different UNCERTAINTIES — the burette (±0.05) is far more precise than the measuring cylinder (±1).
wrong_explanations.3: The measuring cylinder has a LARGER absolute uncertainty (±1 cm³ vs ±0.05 cm³) — it is LESS precise, not more.

quiz.q: When reading a burette or measuring cylinder containing water, where should you read the volume?
opts: From the bottom of the meniscus — water curves downward, creating a concave surface
From the top of the meniscus — this gives the largest volume reading
From the middle of the meniscus — splitting the difference
It doesn't matter — the meniscus reading is always correct

wrong_explanations.1: Reading from the TOP of the meniscus gives an overestimate — the markings are calibrated for the bottom of the meniscus.
wrong_explanations.2: Reading from the middle introduces error — always read from the BOTTOM consistently to match calibration.
wrong_explanations.3: Reading from the wrong part of the meniscus introduces a systematic error — it DOES matter and affects accuracy.

foundation.common_mistake: Read the volume from the BOTTOM of the meniscus — not the top. Water curves downward in a glass tube, creating a concave meniscus. Reading from the top overestimates the volume. Also: zeroing the balance (taring) before each measurement is essential — failing to do so introduces a systematic error.

fifas.label: Percentage Uncertainty
fifas.question: A student measures 25.0 cm³ of solution using a measuring cylinder with an uncertainty of ±0.5 cm³. Calculate the percentage uncertainty.

quantitative/percentage-yield.summary: Calculate percentage yield and explain why the actual yield is often less than the theoretical yield.

quiz.q: A reaction has a theoretical yield of 20 g but only 14 g is obtained. What is the percentage yield?
opts: 143% — (20 ÷ 14) × 100 = 143% (inverted the fraction)
30% — 100% minus 70% (calculated the loss not the yield)

wrong_explanations.1: % yield = actual ÷ theoretical × 100 = 14 ÷ 20 × 100 = 70%, not 43%.
wrong_explanations.2: Must divide actual by theoretical — not theoretical by actual. That gives a value over 100%, which is impossible for yield.
wrong_explanations.3: 30% is the percentage LOSS — the question asks for the percentage YIELD, which is 70%.

quiz.q: Why is the actual yield of a reaction almost always less than the theoretical yield?
opts: Practical losses during separation, incomplete reactions, side reactions and reversible reactions all reduce the amount of product collected
The law of conservation of mass means mass must be lost during reactions
Theoretical yield calculations always overestimate because chemists round up
Products decompose after forming — all products eventually revert to reactants

wrong_explanations.1: Conservation of mass means total mass is conserved — but some mass ends up as by-products, waste or losses, not the desired product.
wrong_explanations.2: Theoretical yield calculations are based on exact stoichiometry — they don't systematically overestimate.
wrong_explanations.3: Not all products decompose — the reasons are specific: losses, incomplete reactions, reversibility, side reactions.

foundation.common_mistake: The theoretical yield must be calculated from the balanced equation — not just assumed. Use moles: find moles of limiting reactant → use molar ratio → calculate mass of product. Then divide actual mass by this calculated theoretical mass.

fifas.label: Percentage Yield
fifas.question: A student expects to make 8.0 g of copper sulfate but only collects 6.2 g. Calculate the percentage yield.

foundation.triple_box: Percentage yield (4.3.3.1) is chemistry-only — not in Combined Science. Students must calculate percentage yield and explain why actual yield is less than theoretical yield.

quiz.q: A reaction has a theoretical yield of 20 g but only 14 g is obtained. What is the percentage yield?
opts: 143% — (20 ÷ 14) × 100 = 143% (inverted the fraction)
30% — 100% minus 70% (calculated the loss not the yield)

wrong_explanations.1: % yield = actual ÷ theoretical × 100 = 14 ÷ 20 × 100 = 70%, not 43%.
wrong_explanations.2: Must divide actual by theoretical — not theoretical by actual. That gives a value over 100%, which is impossible for yield.
wrong_explanations.3: 30% is the percentage LOSS — the question asks for the percentage YIELD, which is 70%.

quiz.q: Why is the actual yield of a reaction almost always less than the theoretical yield?
opts: Practical losses during separation, incomplete reactions, side reactions and reversible reactions all reduce the amount of product collected
The law of conservation of mass means mass must be lost during reactions
Theoretical yield calculations always overestimate because chemists round up
Products decompose after forming — all products eventually revert to reactants

wrong_explanations.1: Conservation of mass means total mass is conserved — but some mass ends up as by-products, waste or losses, not the desired product.
wrong_explanations.2: Theoretical yield calculations are based on exact stoichiometry — they don't systematically overestimate.
wrong_explanations.3: Not all products decompose — the reasons are specific: losses, incomplete reactions, reversibility, side reactions.

higher.common_mistake: The theoretical yield must be calculated from the balanced equation — not just assumed. Use moles: find moles of limiting reactant → use molar ratio → calculate mass of product. Then divide actual mass by this calculated theoretical mass.

Evaluate the economic and environmental importance of maximising percentage yield. Identify the limiting reactant in a reaction and use it to calculate maximum theoretical yield.
higher.triple_box: Percentage yield (4.3.3.1) is chemistry-only — not in Combined Science. Students must calculate percentage yield and explain why actual yield is less than theoretical yield.

quantitative/atom-economy.summary: Calculate atom economy and explain its importance for sustainable and efficient chemistry.

quiz.q: A reaction produces 80 g of desired product and 20 g of waste product. What is the atom economy?
opts: 80% — desired product Mr = 80, total products = 80 + 20 = 100; (80 ÷ 100) × 100 = 80%
20% — (20 ÷ 100) × 100 (calculated waste not yield)
400% — (80 ÷ 20) × 100 (divided desired by waste)
75% — calculated incorrectly

wrong_explanations.1: 20% is the proportion of WASTE — atom economy = desired ÷ total = 80 ÷ 100 = 80%.
wrong_explanations.2: Must divide desired by TOTAL (desired + waste) — not by waste alone.
wrong_explanations.3: No standard formula gives 75% here — atom economy = (80 ÷ 100) × 100 = 80%.

quiz.q: Why do addition reactions always have an atom economy of 100%?
opts: Only one product is formed — all atoms from the reactants end up in the single product with no by-products
Addition reactions are always complete — 100% of reactants are converted to products
Addition reactions release energy — energy counts as a product increasing the total
The reactants have the same Mr as the products — so the ratio is always 1

wrong_explanations.1: 100% atom economy relates to NO by-products — not to whether the reaction is complete. A complete reaction with by-products still has less than 100% atom economy.
wrong_explanations.2: Energy is not counted as a chemical product in atom economy calculations.
wrong_explanations.3: In addition reactions the product Mr equals the sum of reactant Mr exactly — but the key reason is that ALL atoms go into the ONE product.

foundation.common_mistake: Atom economy uses the relative formula masses of the PRODUCTS (from the equation) — not the reactants. Divide the Mr of the DESIRED product by the sum of Mr of ALL products. Do not confuse with percentage yield — atom economy is about the equation; percentage yield is about the actual experiment.

fifas.label: Atom Economy Calculation
fifas.question: Calculate the atom economy for making ethanol (C₂H₅OH, Mr = 46) from the reaction: C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH
steps: Atom economy = (Mr desired product ÷ sum Mr all products) × 100
Only one product: C₂H₅OH, Mr = 46. Sum of all products = 46
Atom economy = 100% — addition reaction, one product only

quantitative/concentration-of-solutions.summary: Define concentration and calculate it from mass and volume.

quiz.q: 25 g of glucose is dissolved in 500 cm³ of water. What is the concentration in g/dm³?
opts: 50 g/dm³ — volume = 500 ÷ 1000 = 0.5 dm³, concentration = 25 ÷ 0.5 = 50
0.05 g/dm³ — concentration = 25 ÷ 500
12500 g/dm³ — concentration = 25 × 500
2.5 g/dm³ — concentration = 25 ÷ 10

wrong_explanations.1: This uses 500 cm³ directly without converting to dm³ — gives an answer 1000× too small. Must convert: 500 cm³ = 0.5 dm³.
wrong_explanations.2: This multiplies instead of divides — and still fails to convert units.
wrong_explanations.3: This divides by 10 instead of 0.5 — an incorrect conversion. 500 cm³ = 0.5 dm³, not 10 dm³.

quiz.q: A solution has a concentration of 80 g/dm³. What mass of solute is in 250 cm³ of this solution?
opts: 20000 g — mass = 80 × 250 (forgetting to convert cm³ to dm³)

wrong_explanations.1: Using 250 directly without converting gives 80 × 250 = 20,000 — not 20. Always convert: 250 cm³ = 0.25 dm³.
wrong_explanations.2: Dividing gives volume, not mass. mass = concentration × volume.
wrong_explanations.3: 250 ÷ 10 = 25 — but the conversion is 250 ÷ 1000 = 0.25 dm³.

foundation.common_mistake: Volume MUST be in dm³ when using the concentration formula. If given in cm³, divide by 1000 first. 250 cm³ = 0.250 dm³. Students frequently forget this conversion and get answers 1000 times too large or too small.

chemical-changes/reactivity-series.summary: Describe the reactivity series of metals and use it to predict reactions.

quiz.q: A strip of zinc is placed in copper sulfate solution. What is observed?
opts: The blue solution fades and copper metal deposits on the zinc — zinc displaces copper
No change — zinc and copper have similar reactivity
The zinc dissolves and the solution turns orange
Zinc sulfate crystals form in the blue solution

wrong_explanations.1: Zinc IS more reactive than copper — a reaction occurs. The solution changes from blue (Cu²⁺) to colourless/pale (Zn²⁺).
wrong_explanations.2: The solution turns pale/colourless as Cu²⁺ ions are replaced by Zn²⁺ — orange is not the colour of any of these solutions.
wrong_explanations.3: Zinc sulfate is formed in solution (dissolved) — it doesn't crystallise out immediately under these conditions.

quiz.q: Why does copper not react with dilute hydrochloric acid?
opts: Copper is BELOW hydrogen in the reactivity series — it cannot displace hydrogen from the acid
Copper is too dense to react with liquids
Hydrochloric acid is not strong enough to react with any metals
Copper reacts with acid but the product is insoluble

wrong_explanations.1: Density doesn't determine reactivity — gold is denser than magnesium but far less reactive.
wrong_explanations.2: HCl reacts with many metals — magnesium, zinc, iron all react vigorously with HCl. Only metals BELOW hydrogen in the series don't react.
wrong_explanations.3: Copper doesn't react with dilute HCl at all — it doesn't produce any product.

foundation.common_mistake: A metal can only displace another metal that is BELOW it in the reactivity series. Copper cannot displace iron from iron sulfate — copper is less reactive than iron. The more reactive metal always displaces the less reactive one, not the other way around.
